risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The ROADMAP study was a randomized, double-blind, prevention study" and Table 3 shows "Placebo" versus "Olmesartan" groups in the parent ROADMAP trial. Comment: The article explicitly identifies the parent study as randomized, and this is the study from which the analyzed cohorts originated; although the exact sequence-generation method is not described in this follow-up paper, the term randomized in this context supports a proper random allocation process rather than a non-random rule.

Summary: The text describes ROADMAP-OFU as a prespecified observational follow-up of patients who had previously participated in the randomized ROADMAP study, but it gives no information on how allocation was concealed in the original randomization. It reports that patients were assigned to cohorts according to their previous study medication (placebo vs olmesartan), but does not describe any concealment mechanism such as central allocation or opaque sealed envelopes. Comment: Because allocation concealment concerns whether recruiters could foresee the next assignment before enrollment, and this information is absent here, the risk is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The full text describes ROADMAP-OFU as a "prespecified, multicenter, longitudinal observational, follow-up" study in which "the patients received standard medical care and were treated at the discretion of the primary physician" and "only medical examinations and treatment according to standard medical care were documented." This follow-up was not an intervention trial and participants/personnel were not delivering a randomized study intervention during OFU. For the outcomes of interest (including diabetic retinopathy, microalbuminuria, cardiovascular and renal events), the lack of study blinding in the observational follow-up is unlikely to create meaningful performance bias relative to the dominant study question, because treatment was routine care rather than protocol-driven allocation during the follow-up period.

Summary: The study was a noninterventional observational follow-up, and the authors state that "the patient treatments varied accordingly and the visits to the physicians were not performed in a standardized way but at variable time points according to local medical standard and patient needs." They also note that "microalbuminuria was detected according to different local standards and was not centrally assessed" and that "CV and renal events reported by the investigators were not adjudicated by an independent endpoint-monitoring committee." Comment: Outcomes were therefore assessed by local investigators/physicians (and for microalbuminuria also local standards rather than centralized blinded assessment), with no evidence of blinded outcome assessment. Because the outcome set includes subjective/judgement-dependent outcomes such as "new onset of diabetic retinopathy" and cardiovascular events reported by investigators, lack of blinded assessment could influence measurement, so the domain is High risk.

Quote: "Out of 2198 eligible patients (placebo group 1104; olmesartan group: 1094) a total of 1758 patients (placebo group: [79.4%]; olmesartan group: [80.6%]) participated in the OFU." Comment: This indicates substantial loss to follow-up before the observational follow-up analysis (about 20% overall; 21.1% vs 19.4% not enrolled), and the paper does not provide a fully convincing missing-data analysis to show robustness. For the analysed follow-up outcomes, only subsets had available data (eg, "In 862 (49.0%) OFU patients ... albumin excretion was measured at least once"), so missing outcome data were extensive for key outcomes. Under the stated thresholds, this is not low-risk and is compatible with attrition bias.
